articles.title: Orange, Bouygues, and Iliad Carve Up SFR in €20.35B Deal That Remakes French Mobile
articles.subtitle: A three-way acquisition collapses France from four operators to three — and reprices European mobile network assets.
articles.body_md:
The most significant structural change to the French mobile market in a generation arrived on 17 April, when **Orange**, **Bouygues Telecom**, and **Free–Iliad Group** jointly entered exclusive negotiations with Altice France to acquire **SFR**'s core assets for **€20.35 billion**.

A previous €17 billion approach was rejected by Altice owner **Patrick Drahi** in October 2025; the revised bid — a 20% uplift — secured exclusivity through May 15, 2026. The deal would reduce France from four mobile operators to three, distributing SFR's assets across the consortium in a surgical split: **Bouygues** absorbs the entire SFR B2B base and rural mobile network (~42% consortium share), gaining the greatest network scale increment. B2C customers, urban spectrum (700 MHz and 3.5 GHz), and physical infrastructure are shared across all three buyers. Notably excluded: **XP Fibre** (SFR's wholesale fibre arm), **Ultraedge** (edge compute), and Altice Technical Services — signalling Drahi retains optionality on infrastructure-light assets that may attract separate bidders.

## Why This Reprices European Mobile Assets

For infrastructure investors, the deal's significance extends well beyond France. Coming weeks after the **VodafoneThree** approval in the UK established regulatory precedent, SFR is the first test of whether European competition authorities will consistently tolerate four-to-three mobile consolidation at scale. The Draghi Report framework — which elevated competitiveness concerns above pure consumer-protection logic — has provided the regulatory cover that operators have been waiting for since 2016. If the European Commission approves the SFR deal without remedies that functionally reconstitute a fourth player, it will open the door to similar transactions in the Netherlands, Belgium, and Spain.

For dealmakers, the €20.35 billion enterprise value sets a new reference price for large-market European mobile operators. Bouygues emerges with the most structurally valuable assets: rural mobile coverage is increasingly scarce, politically protected, and subsidy-eligible — a combination that will command premium multiples as EU digital inclusion requirements tighten.

**EC competition filing is expected in H2 2026.** The critical variable is spectrum remedy design — specifically whether the Commission imposes conditional redistribution of 700 MHz and 3.5 GHz holdings to a new entrant or MVNO, which would dilute the economics of each consortium member. A forced rural infrastructure divestiture to a fourth operator remains a non-trivial risk.
articles.summary: Orange, Bouygues, and Free–Iliad have entered exclusive negotiations to acquire SFR for €20.35B, collapsing France from four mobile operators to three. The deal — if cleared by the EC — reprices French network assets and signals Europe's most permissive consolidation regulatory environment in a generation.
bullets: €20.35B enterprise value — a 20% increase from the €17B bid rejected in October 2025
Bouygues Telecom takes ~42% of consortium and absorbs SFR's entire rural mobile network
Exclusivity window runs until May 15, 2026; France would fall from four to three mobile operators
XP Fibre, Ultraedge, and Altice Technical Services excluded — Altice retains infrastructure-light optionality
EC competition filing expected H2 2026; 700 MHz and 3.5 GHz spectrum redistribution is key remedy risk

sources.title: Bouygues, Iliad, Orange enter exclusive SFR negotiations

articles.title: Italy Moves to Renationalise Telecom Italia in €10.8B Poste Italiane Takeover
articles.subtitle: Three decades after privatisation, the Italian state is set to control 50%+ of TIM — and analysts say the price is still 10% too low.
articles.body_md:
Italy's state-backed postal operator **Poste Italiane** launched a **€10.8 billion** ($12.5B) full takeover bid for **Telecom Italia (TIM)** on 22 March 2026 — effectively initiating the renationalisation of a company privatised three decades ago. The offer: **€0.167 cash** plus **0.0218 newly issued Poste shares** per TIM share, representing a 9.01% premium to TIM's 20 March closing price.

Poste first acquired a minority TIM stake in February 2025, making this the logical endpoint of a publicly telegraphed strategic positioning. Analysts widely estimate the offer price is approximately **10% below fair value** — creating realistic expectation of an improved bid before any deal closes. The structure is deliberately balance-sheet-friendly: the share-plus-cash format preserves Poste's investment-grade credit profile while absorbing TIM's legacy debt load.

## Scale and State Control

The combined entity would generate approximately **€27 billion** in revenue and **€4.8 billion** in EBIT, with a headcount exceeding **150,000**. The Italian government currently holds a 27.3% indirect stake in TIM; post-deal, it would control 50%+ of the combined group — a de facto re-entry of the state into national digital infrastructure. CEO **Del Fante** reaffirmed the strategic rationale in an April 13 Financial Times interview, citing digital infrastructure integration and postal logistics convergence as the primary drivers.

For the European consolidation narrative, timing is striking. Within days, Italy and France both delivered major structural events, confirming **Goldman Sachs**' 2026 thesis that Italy was a near-term consolidation candidate. **TIM NetCo**, the wholesale fibre subsidiary carved out in the KKR transaction, remains an open variable: its regulatory status under a renationalised parent requires clarification from AGCOM, Italy's communications regulator.

For infrastructure investors, the Italian state's re-entry into TIM is a mixed signal. State backing reduces refinancing risk and provides political insulation. But it also introduces strategic objectives — rural coverage mandates, employment protections, public interest obligations — that may constrain financial discipline and return optimisation relative to a purely commercial acquirer.

**Watch:** Whether Poste lifts the offer price toward analyst consensus (implying a final deal value near **€12 billion**), and whether EU antitrust review proceeds concurrently with the SFR filing — a scenario that would give the Commission an unusually concentrated view of European telecom consolidation.
articles.summary: Poste Italiane has launched a €10.8B takeover bid for Telecom Italia, which would put 50%+ of the combined group under Italian state control. Analysts flag the offer as ~10% below fair value, making a price improvement likely before the deal closes.
bullets: €10.8B ($12.5B) bid launched 22 March 2026 — €0.167 cash plus 0.0218 Poste shares per TIM share
9.01% premium to TIM's 20 March closing price; analysts estimate ~10% discount to fair value
Combined entity: ~€27B revenue, ~€4.8B EBIT, 150,000+ headcount
Italian state would hold 50%+ of combined group post-deal (currently 27.3% indirect stake in TIM)
CEO Del Fante confirmed digital infrastructure and postal logistics thesis in FT interview, 13 April

sources.title: Poste Italiane Launches €10.8 Billion Telecom Italia Bid

articles.title: IEA: Data Centres Used 485 TWh in 2025 — Europe Faces 70% Power Surge by 2030
articles.subtitle: The most authoritative energy audit yet puts AI's grid impact in sharp focus, with European TSOs absorbing 65 TWh of incremental demand in under six years.
articles.body_md:
The International Energy Agency published its annual data centre electricity analysis on 22 April, delivering the most authoritative quantification yet of AI's power trajectory. The headline: global data centre consumption reached **485 TWh in 2025**, up **17%** year-on-year — and that was with grid bottlenecks actively constraining growth. For context, overall global power demand grew just 3% in the same period.

## The 2030 Numbers Are Structural, Not Cyclical

The IEA's 2030 projection is unambiguous: consumption doubles to **950 TWh** globally, with AI-specific workloads tripling from current levels. The European figure is the most operationally significant for regional investors: **+45 TWh (+70%)** by 2030 from a 2024 baseline — equivalent to adding approximately one mid-sized country's entire residential electricity load to grids not designed to absorb it in six years. The US adds +240 TWh (+130%) and China +175 TWh (+170%), but European utilities face the constraint of infrastructure built for demand patterns from the 1990s.

Two data points in the IEA report deserve particular attention. First, tech companies signed approximately **40% of all corporate renewable PPAs** in 2025 — cementing hyperscalers as the marginal buyer in European clean power markets and creating structural competition between industrial energy consumers and data centre operators for the same grid-connected renewable capacity. Second, the conditional offtake pipeline for **small modular reactors (SMRs)** grew from **25 GW** at end-2024 to **45 GW** in under four months — a 20 GW increase that confirms nuclear as the preferred 24/7 carbon-free baseload solution for operators who cannot manage intermittency in their power mix.

For European infrastructure funds, the IEA report closes the debate about whether the power constraint is real. It is. The question now is which assets capture the incremental demand premium: grid-adjacent data centre sites with secured interconnection, flexible grid assets (battery storage, demand response), and long-duration energy storage are the three categories most directly repriced by this data. The report also strengthens the investment case for Nordic and Icelandic DC locations where hydropower provides reliable, low-cost, clean baseload — the exact profile the IEA identifies as scarce globally.

**Watch:** Whether European transmission system operators — **National Grid** (UK), **RTE** (France), **TenneT** (Netherlands/Germany) — revise their long-term capacity projections upward at their next planning cycles. TSO investment plans are the earliest leading indicator of where incremental DC supply will be feasible versus constrained.
articles.summary: The IEA quantifies global data centre electricity at 485 TWh in 2025 — up 17% — with Europe alone facing 70% growth by 2030. SMR conditional offtake agreements grew 80% in under four months, confirming nuclear as the preferred baseload solution for AI infrastructure.
bullets: 485 TWh consumed globally by data centres in 2025 — up 17% vs. 3% overall global power demand growth
IEA 2030 projection: 950 TWh global consumption; AI-specific workloads triple
Europe: +45 TWh (+70%) by 2030 from 2024 baseline — roughly 65 TWh of incremental demand on European grids
Tech sector signed ~40% of all corporate renewable PPAs in 2025
SMR conditional offtake pipeline grew from 25 GW to 45 GW in under four months — a 20 GW increase

sources.title: Data centre electricity use surged in 2025
IEA warns AI data centre electricity use will triple by 2030
IEA: AI data centres energy grid concerns

articles.title: Both Subsea Corridors Now Closed: US-Iran Conflict Reaches the Strait of Hormuz
articles.subtitle: IRGC threats against 7 Hormuz cables create an unprecedented dual-chokepoint closure — GCC states running 90%+ of internet and banking through this corridor have no rerouting alternative.
articles.body_md:
The subsea cable disruption this publication has tracked since Edition 1 entered historically unprecedented territory this week. **IRGC**-linked media issued explicit threats against the **7 submarine cables** transiting the **Strait of Hormuz** — the sole data corridor for six GCC states whose internet, banking, and cloud services run through that chokepoint at over **90% dependency**. Combined with the **17 cables** already disrupted in the Red Sea since the Gulf conflict began, commercial repair vessels are now unable to safely access either corridor simultaneously.

## A Dual-Chokepoint Closure With No Historical Precedent

The practical implication of dual-corridor denial is stark. The Red Sea disruption, which began in 2025, triggered rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope at an additional ~**150ms latency** premium — degraded but functional. A Hormuz severing offers no equivalent workaround. The cables running through the Strait serve the UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Oman — collectively the GCC — and carry not only internet traffic but banking settlement infrastructure and enterprise WAN connectivity. For operators with Gulf PoPs, this is an exposure that cannot be rerouted by any existing submarine path.

The repair timeline dimension is equally significant. Any cables severed under current conditions may remain unrepaired for the entire duration of hostilities — an indefinite window. The **WorldLink** cable, the **$700 million** UAE-Iraq hybrid bypass announced in Edition 5 with 900 Tbps capacity and sub-100ms Europe-to-Middle East latency, is still under construction. It cannot mitigate near-term disruption.

For European infrastructure investors with exposure to Gulf-facing operators or Middle East hyperscaler buildout programmes — **AWS Bahrain**, **Microsoft UAE**, **Google Saudi Arabia** — contingency planning now extends beyond degraded performance to complete isolation scenarios. Satellite fallback capacity (Starlink, Intelsat, SES O3b) becomes operationally material for Gulf business continuity in a way it was not six months ago.

The strategic read-across: subsea cable infrastructure is no longer a background operational concern. It is a first-order geopolitical risk factor affecting asset valuations, carrier selection, and colocation pricing for any operator with Gulf or Red Sea routing dependency. The dual-chokepoint scenario explicitly validates the investment thesis behind terrestrial bypass routes and satellite contingency assets.

**Watch:** Whether the conflict timeline allows either corridor to reopen for emergency repair, and whether Gulf hyperscalers accelerate terrestrial fibre or satellite contingency deployment as a structural — rather than reactive — response to corridor-dependent architecture.
articles.summary: IRGC-linked media has explicitly threatened the 7 submarine cables in the Strait of Hormuz, creating an unprecedented scenario where both major subsea cable corridors are simultaneously inaccessible to repair vessels. The six GCC states depend on Hormuz cables for 90%+ of internet and banking services and have no viable rerouting alternative while hostilities continue.
bullets: 7 submarine cables in the Strait of Hormuz explicitly threatened by IRGC-linked media
GCC states (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman) run 90%+ of internet and banking through Hormuz corridor
17 Red Sea cables disrupted since Edition 1; dual-corridor closure has no historical precedent in submarine cable operations
Cape of Good Hope rerouting adds ~150ms latency but is not a viable alternative for Hormuz-transiting cables
WorldLink $700M UAE-Iraq bypass cable remains under construction — not yet operational as contingency

sources.title: IRGC flags risk of severing Hormuz submarine cables
Iran could threaten Gulf data centers and undersea cables
Internet: the next collateral — Iranian media flags new risk at Hormuz

articles.title: EQT Launches €8B GlobalConnect Sale — 244,000km Nordic Fibre, 23 Data Centres, 27 Subsea Cables
articles.subtitle: The largest integrated Nordic digital infrastructure asset to reach the market in years tests whether bundled convergent models command a premium over break-up value.
articles.body_md:
**EQT** has formally launched the sale of **GlobalConnect**, appointing **Goldman Sachs** to manage an auction targeting approximately **€8 billion**. Bidder materials were distributed this week, with **Antin Infrastructure Partners** identified among early-stage suitors. The asset represents EQT's nine-year build: four independent Nordic fibre platforms assembled since 2017 into the region's most integrated digital infrastructure operator.

## What €8B Buys

GlobalConnect's portfolio is unusual in its breadth: **244,000km of fibre** spanning Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Germany, and Finland; **23 data centres**; **27 subsea cables**; connectivity to over **900,000 homes** and **30,000 business customers**. A new Åland subsea cable (Sweden–Finland) is scheduled for completion in 2026, adding live value at a moment when subsea connectivity commands significant premiums amid the Red Sea and Hormuz corridor disruptions tracked in this edition.

The most strategically significant element is EQT's decision to sell GlobalConnect as a **single entity rather than break it up**. This is a deliberate signal: EQT believes the bundled multiple for an integrated fibre-plus-data-centre-plus-subsea operator exceeds a sum-of-parts realisation. For infrastructure fund managers, this framing tests whether convergent digital asset models command platform premiums or attract discounts for operational complexity.

## The Nordic Power Advantage

For infrastructure allocators, GlobalConnect's jurisdictional mix is as important as its asset list. Nordic data centres operate at power costs between **~6p/kWh** (Norway) and **~8p/kWh** (Sweden) — compared to the **24.67p/kWh** industrial electricity cost that prompted **OpenAI** to pause its Stargate UK deployment in Edition 6. At those economics, Nordic DC capacity commands a structural premium over UK or continental European sites competing for the same AI workloads. GlobalConnect's 23 data centres are already embedded in that power cost environment, with grid access and regulatory predictability that European competitors cannot easily replicate.

The €8B asking price implies a substantial multiple on a per-km fibre basis relative to UK altnet transactions — but the combined fibre-DC-subsea model, in a jurisdiction with proven hyperscaler demand, justifies the premium argument. **Antin** brings a proven Nordic infrastructure track record; sovereign wealth and pension fund consortia are also considered likely bidders for an asset of this scale and quality.

**Watch:** Whether the buyer is a pure infrastructure fund preserving the integrated operating model, or a strategic acquirer seeking to extend a European fibre footprint. The final transaction price versus the **$40B Aligned Data Centers** benchmark set in Edition 5 will establish the reference multiple for integrated Nordic digital infrastructure going forward.
articles.summary: EQT has mandated Goldman Sachs to sell GlobalConnect at a ~€8B valuation — the largest integrated Nordic digital infrastructure asset to come to market in years, comprising 244,000km of fibre, 23 data centres, and 27 subsea cables. Antin Infrastructure Partners is among early bidders; EQT is selling the entire business as a single unit.
bullets: €8B target valuation — Goldman Sachs mandated as sale adviser; bidder documents distributed this week
GlobalConnect spans 244,000km of fibre across Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Germany, and Finland
23 data centres, 27 subsea cables, 30,000 business customers, 900,000+ homes connected
New Åland subsea cable (Sweden–Finland) completing 2026 included in portfolio
Antin Infrastructure Partners identified among early-stage bidders; EQT selling as single entity

sources.title: EQT puts GlobalConnect up for sale with €8bn price tag
EQT aims to sell GlobalConnect at EUR 8 bln valuation
